option: Structured Machine-Readable Error Codes
best_for: Any API with more than a handful of endpoints, so clients can branch on `error.code` instead of parsing message strings
avoid_when: A single-endpoint internal script where nobody but the author ever reads the error
tradeoffs: Clients build reliable error-handling logic against stable codes instead of fragile string matching on human-readable messages

option: Operational vs Programmer Error Distinction
best_for: Deciding whether to fail gracefully (operational: bad input, network blip, expected downstream failure) vs. crash loudly (programmer: null reference, broken invariant)
avoid_when: Never skip this distinction — conflating the two either crashes on recoverable errors or silently limps along on real bugs
tradeoffs: Operational errors should return a clean response; programmer errors should be logged loudly and often should crash the process so orchestration restarts it into a known-good state

option: HTTP Status Code Mapping
best_for: Picking 400 (malformed request) vs 422 (well-formed but semantically invalid) vs 409 (conflict) vs 429 (rate limited) vs 503 (temporarily unavailable) precisely rather than defaulting everything to 400 or 500
avoid_when: Internal RPC-style calls where the transport isn't really HTTP semantics-aware — a single error envelope may matter more than status code precision
tradeoffs: Precise status codes let generic HTTP infrastructure (caches, retries, monitoring) behave correctly without reading the response body

option: Never Leak Stack Traces or Internals to the Client
best_for: Every production API response, without exception — return a generic message plus a correlation id, log the detail server-side
avoid_when: Local development, where full error detail speeds up debugging — gate verbose errors behind an environment check, never behind a client-supplied flag
tradeoffs: Prevents leaking implementation details (file paths, library versions, query fragments) that help an attacker; the correlation id still lets you find the real error in logs

option: User-Facing vs Internal Error Message Separation
best_for: Any error a real user might see — translate internal exceptions into a message a non-technical person can act on ('please check your email format', not 'ValidationError at line 42')
avoid_when: Internal admin tools where the audience is engineers who benefit from the raw error
tradeoffs: Keeps the client-facing surface friendly while still logging the full technical detail for debugging

option: Retry with Exponential Backoff and Jitter
best_for: Calls to flaky or rate-limited downstream services (third-party APIs, sometimes your own DB under load)
avoid_when: The failure is guaranteed to be permanent (e.g. 401 unauthorized, 404 not found) — retrying those just wastes time and obscures the real error
tradeoffs: Backoff with jitter avoids a thundering herd of synchronized retries after an outage; must be paired with idempotency (see BE058) so retries can't double-apply a side effect

option: Circuit Breaker for Downstream Calls
best_for: A downstream dependency that's currently failing repeatedly — stop hammering it and fail fast instead, giving it room to recover
avoid_when: Low-traffic internal calls where a circuit breaker's operational complexity isn't justified by the failure volume
tradeoffs: Fails fast once a threshold of recent failures is hit, protecting both your service (no threads/connections tied up waiting) and the struggling downstream service

option: Timeouts on Every Outbound Call
best_for: Every HTTP/DB/queue call your server makes to another service — an unset timeout means one slow dependency can exhaust your entire request pool
avoid_when: Never skip this — there is no valid case for an unbounded outbound call in a request-serving process
tradeoffs: A hung downstream call without a timeout eventually starves your server of connections/threads even though nothing 'crashed'

option: Dead Letter Queue for Failed Async Jobs
best_for: Background job processing (queues, cron-triggered work) where a job that fails repeatedly needs to stop retrying and get flagged for a human
avoid_when: Synchronous request/response paths — DLQs are an async-job-queue concept, not a request-handling one
tradeoffs: Prevents a single poison-pill job from retrying forever and consuming worker capacity; failed jobs land somewhere visible instead of silently vanishing

option: Graceful Shutdown on SIGTERM
best_for: Any long-running server process behind a deploy/orchestration system (Docker, Kubernetes, PM2) that sends SIGTERM before killing a container
avoid_when: Serverless/edge functions with no persistent process to shut down — this pattern doesn't apply there
tradeoffs: Finishes in-flight requests and closes DB connections cleanly before exiting, avoiding dropped requests during every deploy

option: Structured JSON Logging
best_for: Any production service — plain `console.log` strings are unqueryable once you have real log volume
avoid_when: A quick local prototype where you'll delete the logging before shipping anyway
tradeoffs: Structured fields (level, message, requestId, userId, etc.) let your log aggregator filter/alert on real conditions instead of grepping strings

option: Request/Correlation ID Propagation
best_for: Any system with more than one hop (frontend to backend, backend to downstream service) where you'll eventually need to trace one user's request across logs
avoid_when: A single-process script with no distributed calls to trace
tradeoffs: Generate an id at the edge, propagate it through every downstream call and log line, and return it to the client in error responses so a support ticket can reference it

option: Centralized Error Monitoring (Sentry-style)
best_for: Production apps where you need to know about new error types before a user reports them
avoid_when: Local development only — send monitoring events from staging/production, not from every developer's laptop
tradeoffs: Aggregates and deduplicates errors across all instances with stack traces and context, closing the loop that plain logs alone leave open (nobody watches logs in real time)

option: Alert on Error Rate, Not Individual Errors
best_for: Setting up on-call alerting — page a human when the error *rate* crosses a threshold, not on every single occurrence
avoid_when: A truly critical, rare event (e.g. payment webhook signature failure) may warrant an alert on first occurrence — treat those as an explicit exception
tradeoffs: Rate-based alerting avoids paging fatigue from normal background noise while still catching real regressions promptly

option: Validate at the Boundary (Zod / Pydantic / class-validator)
best_for: Every external input: request bodies, query params, env vars, webhook payloads — validate before any business logic touches the data
avoid_when: Never skip this at a true trust boundary — internal function calls within your own already-validated code don't need re-validation at every layer
tradeoffs: Push invalid-shape errors to the edge of the system with a clear, consistent error instead of a confusing failure three layers deep in business logic

option: Fail Fast on Missing/Invalid Config at Startup
best_for: Required environment variables (DB URL, API keys, secrets) — check they exist and are well-formed before the server starts accepting traffic
avoid_when: A genuinely optional config value with a safe default — those shouldn't fail startup
tradeoffs: Crashing immediately on bad config is far easier to diagnose than a mysterious runtime failure hours later when that code path finally executes

option: Consistent Error Envelope Across All Endpoints
best_for: Pairing with BE059 — every error response uses the same shape (`code`, `message`, `requestId`, optional `details`) regardless of which endpoint or module produced it
avoid_when: A deliberately heterogeneous API where different sub-APIs have genuinely different consumers/contracts — document the divergence explicitly if so
tradeoffs: One error-parsing code path on the client instead of one per endpoint; centralize this in the framework's global error-handling middleware, not per-route try/catch

option: Next.js — error.tsx Boundaries plus Route Handler try/catch
best_for: Next.js App Router apps: use `error.tsx` for render-time errors and explicit try/catch inside Route Handlers for API errors, since they're separate error surfaces
avoid_when: Pages Router legacy code — use `_error.js`/`getInitialProps` error handling conventions instead
tradeoffs: App Router's error boundaries don't automatically catch Route Handler errors — both need to be handled explicitly or API errors leak as unhandled 500s

option: Express/Nest.js — Centralized Error-Handling Middleware
best_for: Express: a single error-handling middleware (4-arg signature) as the last app.use(); Nest.js: a global Exception Filter
avoid_when: Per-route try/catch scattered everywhere instead — harder to guarantee every route produces a consistent error envelope
tradeoffs: Guarantees every unhandled error in any route passes through one place that maps it to the standard envelope (BE082) and logs it consistently

option: FastAPI — Exception Handlers plus Pydantic ValidationError Formatting
best_for: FastAPI apps: register `@app.exception_handler()` for domain exceptions and customize the default 422 response shape from Pydantic validation errors to match your error envelope
avoid_when: A tiny script-style FastAPI app with no real client depending on a stable error shape
tradeoffs: FastAPI's default validation error format is verbose and Pydantic-specific by default — reshape it once, centrally, to match BE082 rather than letting every client parse FastAPI's native format

option: Django REST Framework — Custom exception_handler
best_for: DRF apps: override DRF's `EXCEPTION_HANDLER` setting to reshape both DRF's default error format and your own domain exceptions into one consistent envelope
avoid_when: Server-rendered Django views without DRF — use Django's own `handler404`/`handler500` template-based error pages instead
tradeoffs: DRF's default error format differs from a hand-rolled envelope; centralizing this once avoids every viewset re-inventing error formatting

option: Supabase/Postgres — Map DB Error Codes to Friendly Messages
best_for: Any code path that inserts/updates against Supabase/Postgres directly — catch `unique_violation` (23505), `foreign_key_violation` (23503), `check_violation` (23514) and map them to specific, friendly errors instead of a generic 500
avoid_when: Reading data only, with no writes that could hit a constraint
tradeoffs: Postgres error codes are stable and documented — mapping the common ones (uniqueness, FK, check constraints) turns a raw DB error into an actionable client message ('that email is already taken') instead of a leaked 500
